---
title: ""
format:
  pdf:
    mainfont: "Roboto Condensed"
    sansfont: "Roboto Condensed"
    geometry:
      - top=1in
      - bottom=1in
      - left=1in
      - right=1in
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{xcolor}
        \usepackage{fancyhdr}
        \usepackage{tcolorbox}
        \definecolor{earthgreen}{HTML}{2C4A3E}
        \definecolor{earthaccent}{HTML}{5DAE8B}
        \pagestyle{fancy}
        \fancyhf{}
        \fancyhead[L]{\small\textbf{earthUI}}
        \fancyhead[R]{\small User Guide}
        \fancyfoot[C]{\small Page \thepage\ | earthUI User Guide | Confidential \& Proprietary}
        \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0.5pt}
        \renewcommand{\footrulewidth}{0pt}
---

\begin{flushleft}
{\color{earthaccent}\small\textbf{CHAPTER 5}}\\[2pt]
{\Huge\textbf{Subset Filtering}}
\end{flushleft}

\noindent\textcolor{earthaccent}{\rule{\textwidth}{2pt}}

\bigskip

When fitting an Earth model, you often want to use only a portion of your dataset — for example, sales within a certain date range, properties in a specific area, or observations meeting a minimum price threshold. The **subset** parameter in earthUI's Earth Call Parameters section lets you define row filter expressions that control which observations are included in the model fitting process. The remaining rows are excluded from the fit but remain in the dataset for prediction and export.

## Why Use Subset Filtering?

Subset filtering is valuable in several common scenarios:

- **Time-based filtering** — restrict the model to recent sales (e.g., the last 12 months) while keeping older sales in the dataset for comparison
- **Geographic filtering** — limit the model to a specific area code, neighborhood, or district
- **Outlier exclusion** — remove extreme values that may distort the model without permanently deleting them from the data
- **Iterative modeling** — experiment with different subsets to find the combination that produces the best-fitting model

\begin{tcolorbox}[colback=earthgreen!5, colframe=earthgreen!40, title=]
\small\textit{\textbf{Tip:} Subset filtering is non-destructive — filtered-out rows remain in your spreadsheet and are available for prediction after the model is fitted. This makes it safe to experiment freely.}
\end{tcolorbox}

## Entering a Subset Expression Manually

The **subset** text input is located in section **4. Earth Call Parameters** in the sidebar. You can type a filter expression directly using column names from your dataset. The expression must evaluate to `TRUE` or `FALSE` for each row. Examples:

| Expression | Description |
|:-----------|:------------|
| `sale_age < 1000` | Only rows where sale\_age is less than 1000 |
| `age > 20` | Only rows where age exceeds 20 |
| `sale_price > 100000 & age < 50` | Combined condition using AND |
| `area_id == 460` | Only rows in area 460 |
| `area_id != 460` | Exclude area 460 |
| `style == "Ranch"` | Only Ranch-style properties |

Multiple conditions can be combined with `&` (AND) or `|` (OR):

- `sale_age < 365 & sale_price > 50000` — sales within the last year above \$50,000
- `area_id == 100 | area_id == 200` — sales in either area 100 or 200

## Working with Date Columns

If your dataset includes columns of type **Date** or **POSIXct** (date-time), you can filter on them using the `as.Date()` or `as.POSIXct()` wrapper functions:

| Expression | Description |
|:-----------|:------------|
| `sale_date > as.Date("2024-01-01")` | Sales after January 1, 2024 |
| `sale_date >= as.Date("2023-06-15") & sale_date <= as.Date("2024-06-15")` | Sales within a one-year window |
| `close_date > as.POSIXct("2024-01-01")` | For date-time columns |

\begin{tcolorbox}[colback=earthgreen!5, colframe=earthgreen!40, title=]
\small\textit{\textbf{Tip:} Date columns must be wrapped in \texttt{as.Date("...")} or \texttt{as.POSIXct("...")} for comparisons to work correctly. The Build Filter dialog handles this automatically.}
\end{tcolorbox}

## Using the Build Filter Dialog

For users who prefer a guided approach, the **Build filter...** button below the subset text input opens an interactive dialog that constructs the filter expression visually.

### Opening the Dialog

1. Navigate to **4. Earth Call Parameters** in the sidebar
2. Find the **subset** text input near the top of the parameter list
3. Click the **Build filter...** button directly below the text input

### Adding Conditions

The dialog opens with one empty condition row. Each row has four components:

1. **Column** dropdown — select any column from your dataset
2. **Operator** dropdown — choose from `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`, `==`, `!=`
3. **Value** input — adapts automatically to the column type:
   - **Numeric columns** display a number input field
   - **Date / POSIXct columns** display a calendar date picker
   - **Character / Factor columns** display a dropdown of unique values from that column
4. **Remove** button (X) — removes this condition row

Click **+ Add condition** to append additional condition rows.

### Combining Conditions with AND / OR

When you have two or more conditions, a connector appears between each pair of rows:

- **AND** (`&`) — both conditions must be true for a row to be included
- **OR** (`|`) — either condition can be true for a row to be included

You can set the connector independently for each pair of rows, allowing flexible expressions such as:

\begin{center}
\texttt{sale\_age < 365 \& area\_id == 460 | sale\_price > 500000}
\end{center}

### Preview

At the bottom of the dialog, a **Preview** section shows:

- The constructed expression in code format
- The number of rows that match the filter out of the total (e.g., "847 of 1509 rows match")

This lets you verify the filter is selecting the intended subset before applying it.

### Applying the Filter

Click **Apply** to insert the constructed expression into the subset text input and close the dialog. The expression is now active and will be used the next time you click **Fit Earth Model**. Click **Cancel** to discard changes and close the dialog without modifying the subset text input.

\begin{tcolorbox}[colback=earthgreen!5, colframe=earthgreen!40, title=]
\small\textit{\textbf{Tip:} After applying a filter from the dialog, you can still edit the expression manually in the text input to fine-tune it — the dialog and text input work together, not exclusively.}
\end{tcolorbox}

## How Subset Filtering Affects the Workflow

When a subset expression is active:

- **Fitting** — only matching rows are used to build the Earth model. The fit log will report how many rows were selected (e.g., "subset filter: 847 of 1509 rows selected").
- **Predictions** — the fitted model can still predict on all rows, including those excluded by the filter. The Download Output (Excel) export includes predictions for every row in the original dataset.
- **Reports** — the generated report reflects the subset that was used for fitting. The Dataset Description section shows the number of observations used.
- **Settings persistence** — the subset expression is saved with your other settings and restored when you reload the same input file.

## Practical Examples

**Example 1: Recent sales only**
You have 5 years of sales data but want to model only the last 18 months:
```
sale_date > as.Date("2024-09-01")
```

**Example 2: Exclude a problematic area**
Area 999 contains non-arm's-length transactions:
```
area_id != 999
```

**Example 3: Price band filtering**
Focus on the middle market, excluding both very low and very high sales:
```
sale_price >= 100000 & sale_price <= 750000
```

**Example 4: Combined time and geography**
Recent sales in two specific neighborhoods:
```
sale_date > as.Date("2024-01-01") & (neighborhood == "Oakwood" | neighborhood == "Riverside")
```

\begin{tcolorbox}[colback=earthgreen!5, colframe=earthgreen!40, title=]
\small\textit{\textbf{Tip:} Use parentheses to group OR conditions when combining with AND. Without parentheses, \texttt{A \& B | C} is evaluated as \texttt{(A \& B) | C}, which may not be what you intend.}
\end{tcolorbox}
